Define a False Positive (e.g., a legitimate activity flagged as malicious).
Understand the impact of false negatives (missed threats) and false positives (alert fatigue).
Explain strategies to reduce both false negatives and false positives in security systems.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Blue / Red / Purple Teams + Understand Common Exploit Frameworks + Understand the Definition of Risk + False Negative / False Positive Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz evaluates your understanding of Blue, Red, and Purple team roles, common exploit frameworks, the definition of cybersecurity risk, and the concepts of False Negatives and False Positives. It ensures you have a comprehensive view of security operations and risk assessment.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on the distinct roles and collaboration of security teams.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of exploit frameworks and their use in attacks.
Apply the definition of risk to analyze cybersecurity scenarios.
Differentiate between false positives and false negatives and their implications for security monitoring.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand Concept of Defense in Depth
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces the Concept of Defense in Depth, a security strategy where multiple layers of security controls are placed throughout an IT system. It aims to slow down or stop an attacker, even if one layer is breached.
learning_goals: Explain the principle of Defense in Depth and its benefits.
Identify various layers of security controls (e.g., physical, network, host, application, data).
Understand how multiple layers provide cumulative security.
Apply Defense in Depth principles to design a resilient security architecture.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand Backups and Resiliency
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers the importance of Backups and Resiliency in maintaining data availability and business continuity in the face of disasters or attacks. Understanding these concepts is vital for disaster recovery planning.
learning_goals: Explain the importance of data backups for disaster recovery.
Differentiate between various backup strategies (e.g., full, incremental, differential).
Understand the concept of system resiliency and high availability.
Identify RTO (Recovery Time Objective) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective) in disaster recovery.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: True Negative / True Positive
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module explains the concepts of True Negatives and True Positives in security detection systems. Understanding these concepts helps in accurately evaluating the performance of security controls.
learning_goals: Define a True Negative in the context of security detection (e.g., legitimate activity correctly identified as legitimate).
Define a True Positive (e.g., a malicious activity correctly identified as malicious).
Understand how true negatives and true positives contribute to accurate security posture assessment.
Explain the ideal balance between minimizing false alerts and maximizing true detections.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #11
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project requires you to design a comprehensive security architecture for a hypothetical organization, integrating Defense in Depth principles, robust backup strategies, and resilience measures. You'll justify your choices based on risk assessment.
learning_goals: Design a multi-layered security architecture applying Defense in Depth principles.
Develop a disaster recovery plan incorporating various backup and recovery strategies.
Integrate system resiliency and high-availability concepts into the architecture.
Present and justify security design decisions based on identified threats and risks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand Concept of Runbooks
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces the Concept of Runbooks, which are step-by-step procedures used to perform routine or emergency tasks. In cybersecurity, runbooks are crucial for standardized and efficient incident response.
learning_goals: Define a security runbook and its purpose in incident response.
Understand the benefits of standardized procedures for critical tasks.
Identify key components and structure of an effective security runbook.
Develop a simple runbook for a common security incident scenario.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Cyber Kill Chain
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers the Cyber Kill Chain, a framework that outlines the typical stages of a cyberattack, from reconnaissance to exfiltration. Understanding the kill chain helps in identifying defensive opportunities at each stage.
learning_goals: Explain each stage of the Cyber Kill Chain (e.g., Reconnaissance, Weaponization, Delivery, Exploitation).
Understand how attackers progress through these stages to achieve their objectives.
Identify defensive measures and detection points at each stage of the kill chain.
Apply the Cyber Kill Chain model to analyze and respond to security incidents.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand Concept of Runbooks + Cyber Kill Chain Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of security runbooks for standardized incident response and the Cyber Kill Chain framework for analyzing cyberattacks. It ensures you can structure security operations and comprehend attack methodologies.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on the components and benefits of security runbooks.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of each stage of the Cyber Kill Chain.
Identify defensive opportunities at various points in an attack's lifecycle.
Achieve a passing score to progress in incident response and threat analysis.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Basics of Threat Intel, OSINT
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers the Basics of Threat Intelligence and Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT), focusing on how to collect, process, and analyze information about potential threats. It's crucial for proactive defense.
learning_goals: Define Threat Intelligence and OSINT and their relevance to cybersecurity.
Identify various sources of open-source intelligence for threat analysis.
Understand the lifecycle of threat intelligence (direction, collection, processing, analysis, dissemination).
Explain how threat intelligence can inform security decision-making and defense strategies.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand Basics of Forensics
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces the Basics of Forensics in a digital context, covering principles for preserving, collecting, analyzing, and presenting digital evidence. It's essential for incident response and legal investigations.
learning_goals: Understand the fundamental principles of digital forensics (e.g., chain of custody, integrity).
Identify key steps in the forensic process (e.g., acquisition, analysis, reporting).
Explain common forensic tools and techniques for data recovery and analysis.
Recognize the importance of evidence preservation in incident response.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: MFA & 2FA
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson explains Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) and Two-Factor Authentication (2FA), security methods that require users to provide two or more verification factors to gain access to a resource. MFA/2FA significantly enhances account security.
learning_goals: Differentiate between authentication factors (knowledge, possession, inherence).
Explain how MFA and 2FA enhance security over single-factor authentication.
Identify various MFA/2FA methods (e.g., TOTP, push notifications, biometrics).
Understand best practices for implementing and using MFA/2FA.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Honeypots
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers Honeypots, which are security mechanisms designed to detect, deflect, or, in some manner, counteract attempts at unauthorized use of information systems. They are valuable for threat intelligence and early warning.
learning_goals: Define a honeypot and its purpose in cybersecurity.
Differentiate between low-interaction and high-interaction honeypots.
Understand how honeypots can be used for threat intelligence gathering and attacker deception.
Recognize the benefits and limitations of deploying honeypots.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Basics of Threat Intel, OSINT + Understand Basics of Forensics + MFA & 2FA + Honeypots Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of threat intelligence and OSINT, digital forensics basics, Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA/2FA), and honeypots. It ensures you can proactively detect threats, investigate incidents, and strengthen authentication.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on collecting and analyzing threat intelligence and OSINT.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of digital forensics principles and steps.
Explain the mechanisms and benefits of MFA and 2FA.
Understand the deployment and utility of honeypots for security monitoring.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand Handshakes
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers network handshakes, the process of establishing communication between two devices by exchanging a series of signals. Understanding handshakes is fundamental to analyzing network protocols and troubleshooting connectivity.
learning_goals: Explain the concept of a network handshake and its role in establishing connections.
Describe the TCP three-way handshake process in detail.
Understand the handshake mechanisms for other protocols (e.g., SSL/TLS handshake).
Identify common handshake-related issues during network communication.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Basics and Concepts of Threat Hunting
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces the Basics and Concepts of Threat Hunting, a proactive cybersecurity activity that seeks to detect threats that have bypassed existing security controls. It emphasizes hypothesis-driven investigation.
learning_goals: Define threat hunting and differentiate it from traditional security operations.
Understand the motivations and benefits of proactive threat hunting.
Identify common threat hunting methodologies and data sources (e.g., logs, network flows).
Formulate threat hunting hypotheses and outline investigation steps.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Operating System Hardening
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Operating System Hardening, the process of securing an operating system configuration to reduce its attack surface and improve its resilience against threats. It's a critical foundational security practice.
learning_goals: Explain the importance of OS hardening for reducing vulnerabilities.
Identify common hardening techniques for different operating systems (e.g., disabling unnecessary services, patching).
Implement secure configuration baselines and policies.
Understand the impact of hardening on system functionality and performance.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #12
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with hardening a Linux server, implementing threat hunting strategies to identify potential compromises, and analyzing network handshakes to detect anomalies. You'll demonstrate proactive and reactive security skills.
learning_goals: Apply comprehensive OS hardening techniques to a Linux server.
Develop and execute threat hunting hypotheses using system logs and network data.
Analyze network handshakes to identify suspicious or malformed connection attempts.
Document hardening steps, threat hunting findings, and remediation actions.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand CIA Triad
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces the CIA Triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. These are the three core principles of information security that guide security policy and practice. Understanding them is fundamental to any security role.
learning_goals: Define Confidentiality and identify measures to protect it (e.g., encryption, access control).
Define Integrity and identify measures to ensure it (e.g., hashing, digital signatures).
Define Availability and identify measures to maintain it (e.g., redundancy, backups).
Explain how the CIA Triad principles are applied in cybersecurity.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Basics of Vulnerability Management
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers the Basics of Vulnerability Management, a systematic process of identifying, assessing, reporting, and remediating security vulnerabilities in systems and applications. It's crucial for reducing an organization's attack surface.
learning_goals: Explain the lifecycle of vulnerability management (discovery, assessment, remediation, verification).
Identify various methods for vulnerability scanning and assessment.
Understand how to prioritize vulnerabilities based on risk and impact.
Describe common remediation strategies for identified vulnerabilities.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand Concept of Isolation
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces the Concept of Isolation in cybersecurity, which involves separating systems, networks, or processes to limit the scope of compromise. Isolation is a key defense mechanism.
learning_goals: Define isolation and its purpose in cybersecurity.
Identify different types of isolation (e.g., network segmentation, virtual machine isolation, process isolation).
Understand how isolation limits the impact of a security breach.
Explain practical applications of isolation in secure system design.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Understand CIA Triad + Basics of Vulnerability Management + Understand Concept of Isolation Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of the foundational CIA Triad, the systematic process of vulnerability management, and the crucial concept of isolation in cybersecurity. It ensures you grasp core security principles and practices.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of the vulnerability management lifecycle.
Explain various types of isolation and their security benefits.
Achieve a passing score to progress in security architecture and risk mitigation.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Privilege Escalation
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Privilege Escalation, an attack technique where an attacker gains higher levels of access than initially granted. Understanding escalation methods is crucial for both offensive and defensive security.
learning_goals: Define privilege escalation and its importance in post-exploitation.
Differentiate between vertical (root/admin) and horizontal (another user) privilege escalation.
Identify common privilege escalation techniques (e.g., misconfigurations, kernel exploits, weak passwords).
Understand defensive measures to prevent and detect privilege escalation attempts.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Basics of Reverse Engineering
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces the Basics of Reverse Engineering, the process of analyzing a system to determine its technical specifications, usually in the absence of original design documents. In security, it's used to analyze malware and understand software vulnerabilities.
learning_goals: Define reverse engineering and its applications in cybersecurity.
Understand the difference between static and dynamic analysis.
Identify basic tools and techniques used for reverse engineering (e.g., disassemblers, debuggers).
Explain how reverse engineering aids in malware analysis and vulnerability research.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Privilege Escalation + Basics of Reverse Engineering Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of privilege escalation techniques and the basics of reverse engineering. It ensures you grasp critical offensive tactics and fundamental skills for analyzing software and malware.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on privilege escalation methods and defenses.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of reverse engineering concepts and tools.
Identify scenarios for applying reverse engineering in cybersecurity.
Achieve a passing score to progress in advanced exploit analysis and malware investigation.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Basics of IDS and IPS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces the Basics of Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS), security devices that monitor network or system activities for malicious policy violations. They are critical for real-time threat detection and response.
learning_goals: Differentiate between IDS (detection) and IPS (prevention) functionalities.
Explain the different types of IDS/IPS (e.g., network-based, host-based, signature-based, anomaly-based).
Understand how IDS/IPS detect and respond to security threats.
Identify the benefits and limitations of deploying IDS/IPS solutions.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Web Based Attacks and OWASP10
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers Web-Based Attacks and the OWASP Top 10, a standard awareness document for developers and web application security. Understanding these common vulnerabilities is crucial for securing modern web applications.
learning_goals: Identify and explain the OWASP Top 10 web application security risks (e.g., Injection, Broken Authentication).
Understand the mechanisms behind common web-based attacks (e.g., XSS, CSRF, SQL Injection).
Recognize how to prevent and mitigate these vulnerabilities.
Utilize tools and techniques for testing web application security.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Penetration Testing Rules of Engagement
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson explains the critical concept of Penetration Testing Rules of Engagement, which define the scope, limits, and legal aspects of a penetration test. Adhering to these rules ensures ethical and legal security assessments.
learning_goals: Understand the importance of clear Rules of Engagement (RoE) for penetration testing.
Identify key components of an RoE document (e.g., scope, objectives, communication plan).
Explain the legal and ethical considerations in conducting penetration tests.
Recognize the potential consequences of violating RoE and how to avoid them.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Learn how Malware works and Types
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module provides a comprehensive understanding of how Malware works, its various types (e.g., viruses, worms, ransomware, trojans), and their infection vectors. Knowledge of malware is essential for detection, analysis, and prevention.
learning_goals: Define malware and differentiate between its various types and categories.
Understand common malware infection vectors and propagation methods.
Explain the typical stages of a malware attack lifecycle.
Identify techniques for malware detection, analysis, and removal.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #13
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with performing a penetration test on a web application, focusing on identifying and exploiting OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities. You'll adhere to a defined Rules of Engagement document and document your findings, including IDS/IPS evasion techniques.
learning_goals: Conduct a web application penetration test following strict Rules of Engagement.
Identify and exploit at least three OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities.
Demonstrate knowledge of IDS/IPS bypass or evasion techniques.
Produce a professional report detailing vulnerabilities, proof-of-concept, and remediation.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Perimiter vs DMZ vs Segmentation
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson clarifies the distinctions between a Network Perimeter, a Demilitarized Zone (DMZ), and Network Segmentation. Understanding these concepts is vital for designing robust and secure network architectures.
learning_goals: Define a network perimeter and its role in traditional security.
Explain how a DMZ acts as a buffer zone for public-facing services.
Understand the benefits of network segmentation for limiting attack blast radius.
Differentiate the purpose and implementation of each concept in security design.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Authentication vs Authorization
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module clearly defines and differentiates between Authentication and Authorization, two fundamental concepts in access control. Understanding these distinctions is crucial for implementing secure user access.
learning_goals: Define Authentication as the process of verifying a user's identity ('who you are').
Define Authorization as the process of determining what an authenticated user can do ('what you can access').
Understand the sequence and relationship between authentication and authorization.
Identify examples of authentication and authorization mechanisms.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Perimiter vs DMZ vs Segmentation + Authentication vs Authorization Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of network security zones (Perimeter, DMZ, Segmentation) and the fundamental concepts of Authentication vs. Authorization. It ensures you can design secure network access and manage user privileges effectively.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on the purpose and differences of network perimeters, DMZs, and segmentation.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of the distinction between authentication and authorization.
Apply these concepts to design appropriate network access controls and user permissions.
Achieve a passing score to progress in secure network architecture and identity management.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Phishing
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Phishing, a common social engineering attack where attackers attempt to trick individuals into revealing sensitive information or clicking malicious links. Understanding phishing is essential for user education and defense.
learning_goals: Define phishing and identify its common characteristics.
Recognize different types of phishing attacks (e.g., spear phishing, whaling).
Understand the motivations and techniques used by phishers.
Implement strategies to prevent and mitigate phishing attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Salting
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module explains 'Salting' in the context of password security, which involves adding a unique, random string of characters to each password before hashing it. Salting significantly improves the security of stored passwords.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and mechanism of salting passwords.
Understand how salting protects against rainbow table attacks.
Differentiate between salted and unsalted password hashes.
Recognize the importance of unique salts for each password.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Phishing + Salting Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of phishing as a prevalent social engineering attack and the cryptographic technique of salting for secure password storage. It ensures you can identify social engineering threats and implement robust password security.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on identifying and mitigating various phishing attacks.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of password salting and its security benefits.
Understand how salting combats common password cracking techniques.
Achieve a passing score to progress in social engineering defense and cryptographic security.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Hashing
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Hashing, a cryptographic process that transforms input data into a fixed-size string of characters, typically used for data integrity verification and secure password storage. Understanding hashing is fundamental to many security mechanisms.
learning_goals: Explain the concept of hashing and its one-way nature.
Identify common hashing algorithms (e.g., MD5, SHA-256) and their properties.
Understand how hashing is used for data integrity checks and password storage.
Recognize the importance of collision resistance and preimage resistance in hash functions.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Key Exchange
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module explains Key Exchange, a method in cryptography by which cryptographic keys are securely exchanged between two parties. Secure key exchange is essential for establishing encrypted communication channels.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose of key exchange in secure communication.
Understand the principles of common key exchange algorithms (e.g., Diffie-Hellman).
Identify the role of key exchange in protocols like SSL/TLS and SSH.
Recognize the importance of secure key exchange to prevent Man-in-the-Middle attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: cat
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers the 'cat' command (concatenate), a basic Unix/Linux utility used to display file contents, combine files, and create new files. It's a fundamental command for file manipulation and inspection.
learning_goals: Use 'cat' to display the entire content of a file to standard output.
Combine multiple files into a single output or new file.
Create simple text files using 'cat' with redirection.
Understand the limitations of 'cat' for very large files.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: dd
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces the 'dd' command (disk duplicator), a powerful Unix/Linux utility used for copying and converting files, especially raw disk images. It's essential for forensic imaging and data recovery.
learning_goals: Understand the purpose of 'dd' for raw data copying and conversion.
Utilize 'dd' to create forensic images of disks or partitions.
Perform basic data conversion tasks with 'dd'.
Recognize the power and potential danger of 'dd' if used incorrectly.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #14
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with implementing secure password storage using hashing and salting, generating secure keys for communication, and using Linux commands like 'cat' and 'dd' for secure data handling and forensic imaging.
learning_goals: Implement secure password storage utilizing hashing and salting techniques.
Demonstrate a secure key exchange process for encrypted communication.
Utilize the 'cat' command for file inspection and basic manipulation securely.
Perform forensic imaging of a disk using the 'dd' command while maintaining integrity.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Smishing
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Smishing, a type of social engineering attack that uses SMS (text messages) to trick victims into revealing personal information or clicking malicious links. Understanding smishing is vital for mobile security awareness.
learning_goals: Define smishing and recognize its characteristics.
Identify common tactics used in smishing attacks (e.g., fake alerts, prize notifications).
Understand the risks associated with clicking links or sharing info via SMS.
Implement strategies to prevent and mitigate smishing attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Private vs Public Keys
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module explains Private vs. Public Keys, the core components of asymmetric cryptography. Understanding this distinction is fundamental to secure communication, digital signatures, and public key infrastructure (PKI).
learning_goals: Differentiate between public and private keys in asymmetric encryption.
Explain how public keys are used for encryption and private keys for decryption (and vice versa for digital signatures).
Understand the concept of key pairs and their relationship.
Recognize the importance of keeping private keys secure.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: hping
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces 'hping', a command-line tool for crafting and sending custom TCP/IP packets. It's a powerful tool for network testing, firewall auditing, and advanced reconnaissance, offering more control than 'ping' or 'nmap'.
learning_goals: Utilize 'hping' to generate custom TCP/IP packets.
Perform advanced port scanning and firewall testing with 'hping'.
Craft packets for network reconnaissance and host discovery.
Understand the ethical implications of using 'hping' for network probing.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Smishing + Private vs Public Keys + hping Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of smishing as a social engineering attack, the crucial distinction between private and public cryptographic keys, and your proficiency with the advanced network probing tool 'hping'.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on identifying and preventing smishing attacks.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of private vs. public key cryptography.
Apply 'hping' for advanced network reconnaissance and firewall rule testing.
Achieve a passing score to progress in social engineering defense and network security tools.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: head
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers the 'head' command, a Unix/Linux utility used to display the first few lines of a text file. It's useful for quickly inspecting file contents without loading the entire file.
learning_goals: Use 'head' to display the beginning of a file.
Specify the number of lines or bytes to display.
Combine 'head' with other commands using pipes for efficient text processing.
Quickly preview large log files or data sets.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: grep
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces the 'grep' command (global regular expression print), a powerful Unix/Linux utility for searching plain-text data sets for lines that match a regular expression. It's essential for log analysis and data filtering.
learning_goals: Utilize 'grep' to search for specific patterns within files.
Apply regular expressions for advanced pattern matching.
Use 'grep' with various options (e.g., case-insensitive, line numbers, inverted match).
Filter and analyze log files and other text data efficiently.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: head + grep Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your proficiency with the 'head' and 'grep' commands, essential Unix/Linux utilities for text file manipulation and pattern searching. It ensures you can efficiently extract and analyze information from log files and other data sources.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on using 'head' to view file beginnings.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of 'grep' for searching patterns with regular expressions.
Apply both commands effectively for log analysis and data filtering.
Achieve a passing score to progress in command-line data processing.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Shoulder Surfing
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Shoulder Surfing, a physical social engineering technique where an attacker directly observes sensitive information (like passwords) by looking over someone's shoulder. It highlights the importance of physical security.
learning_goals: Define shoulder surfing and how it's executed.
Identify common scenarios where shoulder surfing can occur.
Understand the types of sensitive information vulnerable to this attack.
Implement countermeasures to prevent shoulder surfing (e.g., privacy screens, awareness).

roadmap_checkpoints.label: PKI
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module delves into Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), a system for creating, managing, distributing, using, storing, and revoking digital certificates. PKI is the foundation for secure digital identities and encrypted communication.
learning_goals: Explain the components and hierarchy of a PKI system (CAs, RAs, certificates, CRLs).
Understand how PKI enables trust in digital communication and identities.
Differentiate between various certificate types and their uses.
Recognize the challenges and security considerations in managing a PKI.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Obfuscation
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces Obfuscation, the practice of making something difficult to understand or interpret, typically applied to code or data. In security, it's used by malware to evade detection and by developers to protect intellectual property.
learning_goals: Define obfuscation and its purpose in software and data protection.
Identify common obfuscation techniques (e.g., renaming variables, code packing).
Understand how malware uses obfuscation to evade antivirus and analysis.
Recognize the challenges obfuscation poses for reverse engineering and incident response.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: winhex
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers WinHex, a universal hexadecimal editor for Windows, widely used in computer forensics, data recovery, and IT security. It allows for detailed examination and manipulation of raw data on disks, files, and memory.
learning_goals: Understand the capabilities and use cases of WinHex in digital forensics.
Navigate and interpret data in hexadecimal format.
Perform basic data recovery and file carving operations using WinHex.
Recognize the importance of integrity when working with raw data.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #15
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with analyzing an obfuscated malware sample using reverse engineering techniques, investigating a simulated physical security breach (shoulder surfing), and performing forensic analysis with WinHex and Autopsy on a compromised system.
learning_goals: Apply basic reverse engineering to de-obfuscate and understand malware functionality.
Identify vulnerabilities related to physical security (shoulder surfing) and propose mitigation.
Utilize WinHex to perform low-level data examination and recovery.
Conduct a forensic investigation using Autopsy to analyze disk images and extract evidence.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: autopsy
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers Autopsy, a popular open-source digital forensics platform used to analyze disk images and file systems. It provides a comprehensive GUI for investigating incidents and collecting evidence.
learning_goals: Install and configure Autopsy for forensic investigations.
Create and analyze disk images within Autopsy.
Utilize Autopsy's features for file system analysis, keyword searching, and timeline generation.
Extract and interpret digital evidence from various sources.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Dumpster Diving
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Dumpster Diving, a social engineering technique where attackers search through trash for discarded sensitive information. It highlights the importance of proper data disposal and physical security.
learning_goals: Define dumpster diving and identify common targets (e.g., financial documents, old drives).
Understand the types of information that can be exposed through improper disposal.
Recognize the legal and ethical implications of dumpster diving.
Implement secure data disposal policies to mitigate this threat.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: wireshark
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces Wireshark, the world's foremost network protocol analyzer. It allows users to interactively browse and inspect captured network traffic, making it indispensable for network troubleshooting, analysis, and security auditing.
learning_goals: Install and configure Wireshark for network packet capture and analysis.
Apply display and capture filters to isolate relevant network traffic.
Dissect various network protocols to understand communication details.
Identify anomalies, malicious activity, and protocol errors using Wireshark.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: autopsy + Dumpster Diving + wireshark Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of digital forensic tools like Autopsy, the social engineering threat of Dumpster Diving, and your proficiency with the network protocol analyzer Wireshark. It ensures you can investigate incidents and analyze network traffic.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on using Autopsy for forensic analysis.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of Dumpster Diving prevention strategies.
Apply Wireshark to capture, filter, and analyze network packets.
Achieve a passing score to progress in forensic investigation and network analysis.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: memdump
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers 'memdump' (memory dump), a technique used to extract the contents of a computer's RAM. Memory forensics is crucial for analyzing volatile data, especially in malware analysis and incident response, as it can reveal running processes, open files, and credentials.
learning_goals: Understand the concept of memory dumping and its importance in forensics.
Identify tools and techniques for performing a memory dump.
Explain the types of volatile data that can be extracted from a memory dump.
Recognize the challenges and ethical considerations in memory forensics.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Kill Chain
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module provides a review and deeper dive into the Cyber Kill Chain, a framework that describes the stages of a typical cyberattack. It reinforces understanding of attack methodologies and defensive opportunities.
learning_goals: Recall and articulate each stage of the Cyber Kill Chain in detail.
Analyze advanced attack scenarios through the lens of the Kill Chain.
Identify specific defensive technologies and strategies applicable at each stage.
Develop incident response plans aligned with the Kill Chain framework.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Diamond Model
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces the Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis, a framework for analyzing cyberattacks by mapping relationships between adversaries, capabilities, infrastructure, and victims. It provides a structured approach to threat intelligence.
learning_goals: Explain the four core features of the Diamond Model (Adversary, Capability, Infrastructure, Victim).
Understand the relationships and meta-features (e.g., time, phase, result) within the model.
Apply the Diamond Model to analyze and enrich threat intelligence.
Utilize the model to understand adversary behavior and predict future attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: memdump + Kill Chain + Diamond Model Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of memory dumping for forensic analysis, a deeper knowledge of the Cyber Kill Chain, and the Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis. It ensures you can perform advanced incident investigation and threat intelligence analysis.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on memory dumping techniques and their forensic value.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of the Cyber Kill Chain stages and defensive strategies.
Apply the Diamond Model to analyze and characterize cyber intrusion events.
Achieve a passing score to progress in advanced threat analysis and incident response.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: ATT&CK
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces the MITRE ATT&CK framework, a globally accessible knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques based on real-world observations. It's essential for understanding and mitigating adversary behavior.
learning_goals: Explain the structure and purpose of the MITRE ATT&CK framework.
Identify common adversary tactics and techniques within the framework.
Understand how ATT&CK can be used for threat modeling, blue teaming, and red teaming.
Map security controls to ATT&CK techniques to assess defensive coverage.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Social Engineering
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module provides a comprehensive review and deeper dive into Social Engineering, covering various psychological manipulation techniques used to trick individuals into divulging information or performing actions. It's crucial for understanding human-centric attacks.
learning_goals: Explain the psychological principles behind effective social engineering attacks.
Identify advanced social engineering techniques (e.g., pretexting, quid pro quo, influence tactics).
Understand the motivations and objectives of social engineers.
Develop and implement comprehensive social engineering awareness and prevention programs.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #16
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with developing an incident detection and response strategy based on the MITRE ATT&CK framework, including identifying relevant adversary techniques and mapping defensive controls. You'll also integrate social engineering awareness into your plan.
learning_goals: Utilize the MITRE ATT&CK framework to analyze potential adversary tactics and techniques.
Develop detection rules and response procedures for specific ATT&CK techniques.
Design a security awareness program to mitigate social engineering threats.
Present a comprehensive security strategy that combines technical controls with human factors.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: ISO
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) standards relevant to information security, particularly ISO 27001 and 27002. Understanding ISO standards is vital for establishing robust information security management systems (ISMS).
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and scope of ISO 27001 and ISO 27002.
Understand the key requirements for establishing an Information Security Management System (ISMS).
Identify common controls and best practices outlined in ISO 27002.
Recognize how ISO standards contribute to organizational information security posture.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: RMF
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers the Risk Management Framework (RMF), a structured approach developed by NIST for integrating security and privacy into the system development life cycle. RMF is crucial for federal agencies and increasingly adopted in the private sector.
learning_goals: Explain the seven steps of the NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF).
Understand how RMF integrates security, privacy, and supply chain risk management.
Identify key roles and responsibilities within the RMF process.
Apply RMF principles to manage security risks throughout a system's lifecycle.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Reconnaissance
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson explains Reconnaissance, the initial phase of a cyberattack where an attacker gathers information about a target. Understanding reconnaissance techniques is essential for anticipating and defending against attacks.
learning_goals: Define reconnaissance and its importance in the attack lifecycle.
Differentiate between passive and active reconnaissance techniques.
Identify common tools and methods used for gathering target information (e.g., WHOIS, nslookup, social media).
Implement defensive measures to limit information exposure during reconnaissance.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: ParrotOS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces Parrot Security OS (ParrotOS), a Linux distribution based on Debian, designed for security professionals and ethical hackers. It comes pre-installed with a wide array of tools for penetration testing, forensics, and reverse engineering.
learning_goals: Install and set up ParrotOS in a virtual or physical environment.
Navigate the ParrotOS desktop environment and file system.
Identify and categorize the security tools available in ParrotOS.
Perform basic reconnaissance and vulnerability scanning using ParrotOS tools.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: ISO + RMF + Reconnaissance + ParrotOS Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of ISO and RMF security frameworks, the reconnaissance phase of an attack, and your familiarity with ParrotOS. It ensures you can apply security standards, conduct initial attack intelligence, and use specialized OS tools.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on ISO 27001/27002 and the NIST RMF processes.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of reconnaissance techniques (passive and active).
Identify key tools and functionalities within ParrotOS for security tasks.
Achieve a passing score to progress in security compliance and ethical hacking.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Kali Linux
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces Kali Linux, a popular Debian-based Linux distribution specifically designed for penetration testing and digital forensics. It is widely used by cybersecurity professionals for offensive security tasks.
learning_goals: Install and configure Kali Linux in a virtual or physical environment.
Navigate the Kali Linux desktop environment and file system.
Identify and categorize the vast array of penetration testing tools available in Kali Linux.
Perform basic security assessments using integrated Kali tools.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: NIST
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) cybersecurity publications and frameworks, particularly the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) and various Special Publications (SPs). NIST guidelines are widely adopted for best practices.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and core functions of the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF).
Understand the structure of NIST Special Publications (e.g., SP 800-53, SP 800-61).
Identify key cybersecurity controls and guidelines provided by NIST.
Recognize how NIST standards aid in developing and improving organizational security programs.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Kali Linux + NIST Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your familiarity with Kali Linux as a premier penetration testing platform and your understanding of NIST cybersecurity frameworks and guidelines. It ensures you can leverage powerful tools while adhering to industry-recognized security standards.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on Kali Linux tools and their applications.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and its components.
Understand how to apply NIST guidelines to improve security posture.
Achieve a passing score to progress in offensive security and regulatory compliance.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: CIS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces the Center for Internet Security (CIS) Controls and Benchmarks, a prioritized set of actions to protect organizations from known cyberattack vectors. CIS provides actionable guidance for improving security.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and structure of the CIS Controls.
Understand how CIS Controls provide a prioritized list of security actions.
Identify key CIS Benchmarks for securing various operating systems and applications.
Recognize how CIS guidance aids in reducing cybersecurity risk effectively.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: CSF
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF), a voluntary framework for organizations to manage and reduce cybersecurity risk. It provides a common language and systematic process for cybersecurity risk management.
learning_goals: Explain the five core functions of the NIST CSF (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover).
Understand how the CSF helps organizations assess and improve their cybersecurity posture.
Identify the framework tiers and implementation profiles.
Apply the CSF to develop or enhance a cybersecurity program.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #17
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with conducting a simulated vulnerability assessment on a target system using Kali Linux, then mapping your findings to both the CIS Controls and NIST Cybersecurity Framework. You'll propose prioritized remediation actions.
learning_goals: Utilize Kali Linux tools to perform a comprehensive vulnerability assessment.
Map identified vulnerabilities to specific CIS Controls and NIST CSF functions.
Prioritize remediation efforts based on risk and impact as per framework guidelines.
Generate a report detailing findings, framework alignment, and actionable recommendations.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Watering Hole Attack
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers the Watering Hole Attack, a specific type of social engineering attack where attackers compromise a website frequently visited by their target group. Understanding this attack vector is important for web security and user awareness.
learning_goals: Define a watering hole attack and its characteristics.
Understand the motivation behind targeting specific websites and user groups.
Identify the stages of a watering hole attack (reconnaissance, compromise, infection).
Implement defensive strategies to mitigate watering hole attacks (e.g., patch management, traffic monitoring).

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Drive by Attack
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces Drive-by Attacks, which occur when a user visits a compromised website that automatically downloads malicious code onto their system without their knowledge or consent. Understanding drive-by downloads is critical for endpoint protection.
learning_goals: Define a drive-by attack and differentiate it from other web-based attacks.
Understand the mechanisms of how malicious code is silently downloaded.
Identify common vectors for drive-by attacks (e.g., malvertising, compromised legitimate sites).
Implement security measures to prevent drive-by downloads (e.g., browser security, endpoint protection).

roadmap_checkpoints.label: SIEM
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson explains Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems, which aggregate and analyze security-related data from various sources across an organization. SIEMs are central to security operations centers for threat detection and compliance.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and core functions of a SIEM system.
Understand how SIEMs collect, normalize, and correlate security event data.
Identify the benefits of SIEM for real-time threat detection and compliance reporting.
Recognize challenges in SIEM implementation and optimization.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: SOAR
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) platforms, which integrate and automate security tasks, workflows, and incident response processes. SOAR enhances the efficiency and effectiveness of security operations.
learning_goals: Define SOAR and its role in modern security operations.
Understand how SOAR platforms orchestrate security tools and automate repetitive tasks.
Identify the benefits of SOAR for incident response and threat management.
Recognize how SOAR improves the efficiency and consistency of security operations.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Watering Hole Attack + Drive by Attack + SIEM + SOAR Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of advanced web attack vectors (Watering Hole, Drive-by Attacks) and core security operations technologies (SIEM, SOAR). It ensures you can analyze sophisticated threats and leverage powerful detection and response platforms.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on the mechanisms and defenses against watering hole and drive-by attacks.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of SIEM functionalities for log management and threat detection.
Explain the benefits and automation capabilities of SOAR platforms.
Achieve a passing score to progress in advanced threat analysis and security operations.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: LOLBAS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers LOLBAS (Living Off The Land Binaries and Scripts), legitimate operating system tools that can be misused by attackers for malicious purposes (e.g., execution, persistence, privilege escalation). Understanding LOLBAS is crucial for detecting stealthy attacks.
learning_goals: Define LOLBAS and explain why attackers use legitimate tools for malicious ends.
Identify common Windows binaries and scripts that can be abused.
Understand the tactics and techniques associated with LOLBAS attacks.
Implement detection and mitigation strategies for LOLBAS usage.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: GTFOBINS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces GTFOBINS, a curated list of Unix binaries that can be exploited by attackers to bypass local security restrictions. It's a valuable resource for privilege escalation and living off the land techniques in Linux environments.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose of GTFOBINS as a resource for Unix binary exploitation.
Identify common Unix binaries and their potential for misuse.
Understand techniques like SUID/SGID exploitation and file descriptor manipulation.
Utilize GTFOBINS to identify and mitigate local privilege escalation vectors.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: WADCOMS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module explores WADCOMS (Windows Active Directory COM Objects), which refers to Windows binaries, scripts, and COM objects that can be abused for various malicious activities, particularly in Active Directory environments. Similar to GTFOBINS but for Windows.
learning_goals: Understand how legitimate Windows components can be abused for attacks.
Identify specific Windows Active Directory COM objects or scripts with exploit potential.
Explain the use of WADCOMS for privilege escalation or persistence.
Recognize defensive strategies to monitor and restrict WADCOMS abuse.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: LOLBAS + GTFOBINS + WADCOMS Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of Living Off The Land Binaries and Scripts (LOLBAS), GTFOBINS for Unix, and WADCOMS for Windows, which are critical for detecting advanced, stealthy attacks. It ensures you can identify legitimate tools used maliciously.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on LOLBAS techniques across operating systems.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of GTFOBINS and its application in Linux exploitation.
Understand the abuse of WADCOMS for Windows-based attacks, particularly in AD.
Achieve a passing score to progress in advanced threat detection and defense.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Typo Squatting
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Typo Squatting (or URL hijacking), a form of cybersquatting where attackers register domain names similar to popular legitimate websites to trick users into visiting malicious sites. Understanding it is crucial for brand protection and user awareness.
learning_goals: Define typo squatting and its common techniques.
Understand how typo squatting can be used for phishing, malware distribution, or ad revenue.
Identify common variations used in typo squatting (e.g., common typos, adjacent keys).
Implement strategies to detect and mitigate typo squatting attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Data Persistence
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module explains Data Persistence, the concept of ensuring that data outlives the process that created it. In cybersecurity, attackers use persistence mechanisms to maintain access to a compromised system across reboots or after initial exploitation.
learning_goals: Define data persistence and its importance in both legitimate applications and cyberattacks.
Identify common attacker persistence mechanisms (e.g., registry keys, scheduled tasks, startup folders).
Understand how persistence contributes to long-term access for adversaries.
Implement defensive strategies to detect and remove persistence mechanisms.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #18
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with implementing and detecting various data persistence mechanisms on a target system, alongside identifying and defending against typo squatting attempts. You'll demonstrate skills in maintaining and preventing unauthorized access.
learning_goals: Successfully implement at least three different data persistence mechanisms on a target system.
Develop detection rules and mitigation strategies for various persistence techniques.
Analyze domain names to identify potential typo squatting attempts.
Propose and implement countermeasures for typo squatting attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Brute Force vs Password Spray
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson differentiates between Brute Force and Password Spray attacks, two common methods used to crack passwords. Understanding their mechanics and differences is key to implementing effective password security.
learning_goals: Define a brute-force attack and its characteristic high volume, low success rate attempts.
Define a password spray attack and its characteristic low volume, high target attempts.
Understand the scenarios where each attack method is effective.
Identify defensive measures against both brute-force and password spray attacks (e.g., account lockout, MFA).

roadmap_checkpoints.label: FTP vs SFTP
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module reviews and clarifies the differences between FTP (File Transfer Protocol) and SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol). It emphasizes the security advantages of SFTP for transferring files.
learning_goals: Recall the core functionalities of both FTP and SFTP.
Clearly articulate the security differences and benefits of SFTP over FTP.
Understand when to use each protocol based on security requirements.
Explain how SFTP leverages SSH for encryption and authentication.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: SSL vs TLS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson reviews and distinguishes between SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) and TLS (Transport Layer Security), focusing on their evolution and security improvements. Understanding their differences is crucial for secure communication.
learning_goals: Recall the historical relationship between SSL and TLS.
Identify the key security improvements of TLS over SSL.
Understand the common use cases for SSL/TLS in securing data in transit.
Explain the importance of using modern TLS versions for strong encryption.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: IPSEC
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers IPsec (Internet Protocol Security), a suite of protocols for securing IP communications by authenticating and encrypting each IP packet in a data stream. IPsec is widely used for VPNs.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and core components of IPsec (AH, ESP, IKE).
Understand how IPsec provides confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity for IP packets.
Differentiate between IPsec tunnel mode and transport mode.
Recognize common applications of IPsec, particularly in VPNs.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Brute Force vs Password Spray + FTP vs SFTP + SSL vs TLS + IPSEC Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz comprehensively assesses your understanding of password attack methods (Brute Force vs. Password Spray), the differences between file transfer protocols (FTP vs. SFTP), encryption protocols (SSL vs. TLS), and the IPsec suite. It ensures a strong grasp of network and authentication security.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions differentiating brute force and password spray attacks and their defenses.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of FTP and SFTP security differences.
Explain the evolution and security features of SSL and TLS.
Understand IPsec protocols and their role in securing IP communication.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: syslogs
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces 'syslogs', a standard for message logging that allows disparate system components and applications to send event notifications to a central logging server. Analyzing syslogs is critical for security monitoring and incident response.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and architecture of syslog for centralized logging.
Understand the different components of a syslog message (e.g., facility, severity).
Configure systems to send syslog messages to a remote server.
Utilize syslog data for security monitoring and incident investigation.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: netflow
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers 'netflow', a network protocol developed by Cisco that collects IP traffic information as it enters or exits an interface. NetFlow provides valuable insights into network usage, performance, and security threats.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and functionality of NetFlow for network monitoring.
Understand the types of information captured by NetFlow records.
Identify how NetFlow data can be used for network forensics and anomaly detection.
Recognize the benefits of NetFlow for security and capacity planning.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Firewall Logs
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson focuses on Firewall Logs, which record information about network traffic that firewalls have allowed or denied. Analyzing firewall logs is essential for understanding network activity, detecting threats, and ensuring security policy enforcement.
learning_goals: Understand the types of information recorded in firewall logs (e.g., source/destination IP, port, action).
Explain how firewall logs are used for security monitoring and auditing.
Identify malicious or suspicious activity by analyzing firewall log entries.
Configure firewalls to generate appropriate and useful log data.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: syslogs + netflow + Firewall Logs Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of syslog for centralized logging, NetFlow for network traffic analysis, and firewall logs for security monitoring. It ensures you can effectively gather and analyze diverse log data for incident detection and investigation.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on syslog architecture and message interpretation.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of NetFlow data and its security applications.
Analyze firewall logs to identify policy violations and malicious activity.
Achieve a passing score to progress in security logging and monitoring.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: DNSSEC
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions), a suite of IETF specifications for securing certain kinds of information provided by the Domain Name System (DNS). DNSSEC adds authenticity and integrity to DNS data.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose of DNSSEC in addressing DNS vulnerabilities (e.g., cache poisoning).
Understand how DNSSEC uses digital signatures to authenticate DNS data.
Identify the key components of a DNSSEC-protected zone.
Recognize the benefits of DNSSEC for preventing DNS-based attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: LDAPS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces LDAPS (LDAP over SSL), which is the Lightweight Directory Access Protocol secured using SSL/TLS. LDAPS provides encrypted and authenticated communication for directory services, enhancing security over plain LDAP.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and benefits of LDAPS over standard LDAP.
Understand how SSL/TLS encryption is applied to LDAP communication.
Identify the default port and configuration requirements for LDAPS.
Recognize the importance of LDAPS for securing sensitive directory information.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: SRTP
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces SRTP (Secure Real-time Transport Protocol), a profile for RTP (Real-time Transport Protocol) that adds security features like message authentication, integrity, and confidentiality to real-time communication. It's crucial for securing VoIP and video conferencing.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose of SRTP in securing real-time audio and video communication.
Understand how SRTP provides encryption, authentication, and integrity to RTP streams.
Identify common applications of SRTP (e.g., VoIP, video conferencing).
Recognize the security benefits of SRTP over unencrypted RTP.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #19
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with configuring and analyzing secure DNS (DNSSEC), LDAP (LDAPS), and real-time communication (SRTP) protocols. You'll ensure data integrity and confidentiality across various critical services.
learning_goals: Successfully implement and verify DNSSEC for a domain or zone.
Configure LDAPS to secure directory service communication.
Set up and test SRTP for encrypted real-time media streams.
Demonstrate how each protocol enhances the security and integrity of its respective service.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: S/MIME
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers S/MIME (Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions), a standard for public key encryption and signing of MIME data, commonly used for securing email communication. S/MIME ensures email confidentiality, integrity, and non-repudiation.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose of S/MIME in securing email communications.
Understand how S/MIME uses digital certificates for encryption and digital signatures.
Differentiate between S/MIME's confidentiality and integrity features.
Recognize the benefits of S/MIME for secure email exchange in enterprise environments.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: DoS vs DDoS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module differentiates between Denial of Service (DoS) and Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks. Understanding these attack types is crucial for network resilience and incident response.
learning_goals: Define a DoS attack and identify its characteristic single-source origin.
Define a DDoS attack and identify its characteristic multiple-source, distributed origin.
Understand the goals and impact of DoS/DDoS attacks.
Identify common DoS/DDoS attack vectors and mitigation strategies.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: S/MIME + DoS vs DDoS Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of S/MIME for secure email communication and the crucial distinction between DoS and DDoS attacks. It ensures you can implement secure messaging and defend against service disruption threats.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on S/MIME's role in email encryption and digital signatures.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of DoS and DDoS attack characteristics and impacts.
Identify effective mitigation strategies for both DoS and DDoS attacks.
Achieve a passing score to progress in secure communication and network resilience.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Spoofing
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers Spoofing, a malicious act where an attacker disguises themselves as a legitimate entity by falsifying data to gain an advantage. Understanding various types of spoofing is critical for network and identity security.
learning_goals: Define spoofing and its purpose in cyberattacks.
Identify different types of spoofing (e.g., IP spoofing, MAC spoofing, email spoofing).
Understand how spoofing can be used to bypass security controls or deceive users.
Implement defensive measures to detect and prevent spoofing attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Antivirus
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces Antivirus (AV) software, which detects, prevents, and removes malicious software from computer systems. Understanding AV is a fundamental aspect of endpoint protection.
learning_goals: Explain the primary function and mechanisms of antivirus software.
Differentiate between signature-based and heuristic/behavioral detection.
Understand the importance of keeping antivirus definitions updated.
Recognize the limitations of traditional antivirus solutions.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Antimalware
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson clarifies Antimalware, a broader term encompassing software designed to protect against all forms of malicious software, including viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, and spyware. It often includes antivirus capabilities.
learning_goals: Define antimalware and its comprehensive scope compared to antivirus.
Identify various types of threats that antimalware solutions target.
Understand the layered approach of antimalware protection (e.g., real-time scanning, behavioral analysis).
Recognize the importance of an integrated antimalware strategy.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: EDR
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solutions, which continuously monitor endpoint activity, detect and investigate suspicious behaviors, and respond to threats in real-time. EDR provides advanced endpoint protection beyond traditional AV.
learning_goals: Define EDR and differentiate it from traditional antivirus/antimalware.
Understand the core capabilities of EDR (e.g., continuous monitoring, threat detection, investigation, response).
Explain how EDR provides deep visibility into endpoint activities.
Recognize the benefits of EDR for proactive threat hunting and rapid incident response.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Spoofing + Antivirus + Antimalware + EDR Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of various spoofing techniques and critical endpoint security solutions (Antivirus, Antimalware, EDR). It ensures you can identify impersonation threats and deploy comprehensive endpoint protection.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on identifying and preventing different types of spoofing attacks.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of Antivirus and Antimalware functionalities.
Explain the advanced capabilities and benefits of EDR solutions for endpoint security.
Achieve a passing score to progress in endpoint protection and threat detection.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: DLP
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces Data Loss Prevention (DLP), a set of tools and processes designed to ensure that sensitive data is not lost, misused, or accessed by unauthorized users. DLP is crucial for protecting confidential information.
learning_goals: Define DLP and its primary goal in data security.
Understand different types of sensitive data that DLP solutions protect (e.g., PII, PCI, HIPAA data).
Identify various DLP deployment methods (e.g., endpoint, network, cloud).
Recognize the importance of DLP policies in enforcing data handling rules.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: JSON / XML
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) and XML (Extensible Markup Language), two common data interchange formats used for transmitting structured data. Understanding these formats is essential for API security, web application security, and log parsing.
learning_goals: Differentiate between JSON and XML syntax and structures.
Understand how JSON and XML are used for data serialization and exchange.
Identify common security vulnerabilities associated with JSON/XML parsing (e.g., XXE, injection).
Parse and generate JSON/XML data in scripts or applications.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #20
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with implementing Data Loss Prevention (DLP) policies to protect sensitive data and securing data interchange using JSON/XML in an application. You'll demonstrate skills in data security and secure data communication.
learning_goals: Design and implement DLP policies to prevent unauthorized data exfiltration.
Configure a system to identify and block sensitive data transfers.
Secure an application's use of JSON/XML data interchange against common vulnerabilities.
Validate the effectiveness of DLP and secure data formatting through testing.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: NAC-based
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers NAC-based (Network Access Control) security, which restricts the availability of network resources to endpoint devices that comply with a defined security policy. NAC is crucial for controlling who and what can connect to a network.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and benefits of Network Access Control (NAC).
Understand how NAC enforces security policies for devices connecting to a network.
Differentiate between pre-admission and post-admission NAC enforcement.
Identify key components and deployment models of NAC solutions.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Evil Twin
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces the Evil Twin attack, a rogue wireless access point that masquerades as a legitimate one to trick users into connecting to it. Understanding this attack is vital for wireless network security.
learning_goals: Define an Evil Twin attack and how it operates.
Understand how attackers set up fake access points to intercept traffic.
Identify indicators of an Evil Twin attack.
Implement defensive measures against Evil Twin attacks (e.g., strong authentication, VPNs).

roadmap_checkpoints.label: NAC-based + Evil Twin Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of Network Access Control (NAC) for enforcing network security policies and the Evil Twin attack as a significant wireless threat. It ensures you can secure network access and defend against rogue Wi-Fi.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on NAC principles, deployment, and enforcement.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of how Evil Twin attacks work and their detection.
Identify strategies to mitigate both unauthorized network access and malicious Wi-Fi.
Achieve a passing score to progress in network access security.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Firewall & Nextgen Firewall
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers traditional and Next-Generation Firewalls, network security devices that monitor and filter incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organization's previously established security policies. Understanding firewalls is fundamental for network defense.
learning_goals: Differentiate between traditional packet-filtering firewalls and Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFWs).
Understand the advanced capabilities of NGFWs (e.g., application awareness, IPS, deep packet inspection).
Configure basic firewall rules to control network traffic.
Explain the strategic placement and role of firewalls in network security architecture.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: HIPS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces HIPS (Host-based Intrusion Prevention System), security software that monitors and protects an individual host from malicious activities and unauthorized changes. HIPS provides an additional layer of security on endpoints.
learning_goals: Define HIPS and its role in endpoint protection.
Differentiate HIPS from network-based intrusion prevention systems (NIPS).
Understand how HIPS detects and prevents malicious activities on a host.
Identify the benefits and challenges of deploying HIPS.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: NIDS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers NIDS (Network Intrusion Detection System), a system that monitors network traffic for suspicious activity and known threats, generating alerts when detected. NIDS is crucial for detecting network-level attacks.
learning_goals: Define NIDS and its role in network security monitoring.
Explain how NIDS identifies malicious patterns (e.g., signature-based, anomaly-based).
Understand the placement and limitations of NIDS in a network.
Interpret NIDS alerts and integrate them into incident response workflows.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Group Policy
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces Group Policy (GP) in Windows environments, a feature that provides centralized management and configuration of operating systems, applications, and user settings. GP is essential for enforcing security policies across an enterprise.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and components of Group Policy in Windows domains.
Understand how Group Policy Objects (GPOs) are created, linked, and enforced.
Identify common security settings configurable via Group Policy (e.g., password policies, firewall rules).
Apply Group Policy to harden Windows systems and enforce security baselines.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Firewall & Nextgen Firewall + HIPS + NIDS + Group Policy Quiz
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This quiz assesses your understanding of Firewalls (traditional and Next-Gen), Host-based IPS (HIPS), Network IDS (NIDS), and Windows Group Policy. It ensures you can implement comprehensive network and host security controls in enterprise environments.
learning_goals: Successfully answer questions on firewall types, functionalities, and configuration.
Demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of HIPS and NIDS for intrusion detection and prevention.
Apply Group Policy to enforce security policies and harden Windows systems.
Achieve a passing score to progress in enterprise security architecture and management.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: DNS Poisoning
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson covers DNS Poisoning (or cache poisoning), an attack where corrupted DNS data is introduced into a DNS resolver's cache, causing the resolver to return an incorrect IP address. This can redirect users to malicious websites.
learning_goals: Define DNS poisoning and explain its mechanism.
Understand how poisoned DNS records can redirect traffic to malicious sites.
Identify common vectors for DNS poisoning attacks.
Implement defensive measures against DNS poisoning (e.g., DNSSEC, secure DNS resolvers).

roadmap_checkpoints.label: NIPS
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module introduces NIPS (Network Intrusion Prevention System), a security device that monitors network traffic for suspicious activity and actively blocks or prevents detected threats in real-time. NIPS is a proactive defense mechanism.
learning_goals: Define NIPS and differentiate it from NIDS (detection only).
Understand how NIPS actively intervenes to prevent network-based attacks.
Identify common deployment considerations and challenges for NIPS.
Explain the benefits of NIPS for automated threat response.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Host Based Firewall
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson focuses on Host-Based Firewalls, software firewalls that run directly on individual hosts (computers) to control network traffic to and from that specific device. They provide granular protection at the endpoint level.
learning_goals: Explain the purpose and functionality of host-based firewalls.
Differentiate between host-based and network-based firewalls.
Configure rules on host-based firewalls (e.g., Windows Firewall, iptables).
Understand how host-based firewalls enhance endpoint security.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Security Engineer Project #21
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This project tasks you with configuring host-based firewalls and implementing network intrusion prevention (NIPS) rules to defend against DNS poisoning and other network-level attacks. You'll demonstrate skills in both endpoint and network security enforcement.
learning_goals: Successfully configure host-based firewalls on multiple operating systems.
Deploy and configure NIPS to detect and prevent DNS poisoning attacks.
Develop custom NIPS rules to block specific malicious network traffic patterns.
Verify the effectiveness of firewall and NIPS rules through simulated attacks.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: Sandboxing
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This lesson introduces Sandboxing, a security mechanism for running programs in an isolated environment, restricting their access to system resources. Sandboxing is crucial for analyzing untrusted code and mitigating zero-day exploits.
learning_goals: Define sandboxing and its purpose in cybersecurity.
Understand how sandboxing isolates processes and restricts resource access.
Identify common applications of sandboxing (e.g., malware analysis, web browser security).
Explain the benefits of sandboxing for containing potential threats.

roadmap_checkpoints.label: ACLs
roadmap_checkpoints.description: This module covers Access Control Lists (ACLs), a list of permissions associated with an object (e.g., a file, directory, or network device) that specifies which users or system processes are granted access to the object and what operations they can perform.
